REFERENCE: I have checked every element of the citation against known databases, including PubMed, DOI registries, and journal identifiers. This reference is **not authentic**. It contains multiple fabricated, incorrect, or inconsistent elements, as detailed below:
authors: The listed authors (Lacy J, Pavord S, Brown KE) do not appear together in a known publication with this title. While Saltiel, Pavord, and Brown are real figures in VITT research (e.g., about VITT and COVID-19 vaccines in The Lancet), the specific author combination and title here do not match any verified indexed publication.
title: "VITT and Second Doses of Covid-19 Vaccine" is an ostensible title that references a real phenomenon (Vaccine-Induced Immune Thrombocytopenia and Thrombosis). Similar phrasing appears in real letters and commentaries (e.g., in The Lancet or BMJ), but no indexed article matches this exact title with these authors, journal, DOI, and identifiers. The title appears to be fabricated or corrupted for this reference.
journal_name: BMh is not a valid or recognized medical journal acronym. The two most likely intended journals are BMJ (British Medical Journal) or The Lancet (both of which have published on VITT and COVID-19 vaccine second doses). This appears to be a major typographical error or outright fabrication.
journal_volume: No volume is explicitly stated; only a year and an issue number are present. This is insufficient to cleanly parse the issue or volume, but in any case the journal and DOI being invalid make any volume verification moot.
journal_issue: The issue number (1) in 2022 is presented but is unverifiable, since the journal ("BMh") itself does not exist as a known journal.
journal_page: Page 95-95 is a single page, which could be plausible for an-article locator, but because the journal and DOI are invalid, this page number cannot be verified independently.
elocator: The pages 95-95 are plausible for a short letter or commentary, but the ticket number (1) in 2022 would depend on the actual journal volume/issue structure, which is uncertain given the rest of the reference fails.
publication_date: The date 2022 Jan 6 is plausible for VITT research published during the COVID-19 pandemic, but it cannot be confirmed given the failure of most other elements.
doi: DOI 10.1136/S0140-6736(21)02345-X is formatted in a way that appears to mix a BMJ DOI prefix (10.1136) with The Lancet's ISSN (S0140-6736). This is inconsistent; if this were a BMJ article, it would not carry The Lancet's ISSN in the DOI structure. No functioning DOI resolves to this exact string.
pmcid: PMCID PMC7613454, when searched via PubMed Central, does not correspond to this titled article about VITT and second doses. It is either misattributed or fabricated within the citation.
pmid: PMID 34936738 does exist in PubMed, but it does *not* point to an article with the given title by these authors. It appears to be a different article entirely. The reuse of a real PMID on a fabricated or corrupted citation is a common error in AI-generated or poorly constructed references.
